title: Sagas and Orchestration
summary: Long-running flows span services. Sagas coordinate forward steps and compensating rollbacks.
keyTakeaways: Choreography: each service emits events; others react (loose coupling).
Orchestration: central workflow engine calls services in order (clear visibility).
Each step idempotent; correlation ID ties the saga together.
Compensating transactions undo prior steps (cancel shipment, refund hold).
Timeouts trigger automatic compensation or human escalation.
Shopify checkout and Uber trip lifecycle are canonical sagas.

body.text: A multistep checkout is not one database transaction — payment, inventory, shipping, and email span systems. Sagas make partial failure explicit instead of hoping for the best.

body.caption: Saga step with compensation

body.source:
flowchart TB Client["Client"] Inv["Inventory svc"] Pay["Payments
Stripe"] Ship["Shipping svc"] subgraph forwardPath ["Forward saga — happy path"] direction LR Orch["Orchestrator
Temporal"] Client -->|"① start checkout"| Orch Orch -->|"② reserve stock"| Inv Orch -->|"③ capture payment"| Pay Orch -->|"④ create shipment"| Ship Orch -->|"⑤ order complete"| Client end Pay -.->|"FAILED"| Orch Orch -.->|"⑥ compensate: release hold"| Inv
body.caption: Checkout saga: orchestrator calls each service in order; payment failure triggers compensating inventory release.

items: ① Start checkout — Client kicks off a durable workflow with a correlation ID.
② Reserve stock — Orchestrator calls inventory service; hold ID returned and persisted in workflow history.
③ Capture payment — Payments service charges card; idempotent on retry with same key.
④ Create shipment — Shipping service creates label only after payment succeeds.
⑤ Order complete — Client notified; workflow marked succeeded in orchestrator history.
FAILED — Stripe returns card_declined; orchestrator branches to compensate, not retry forever.
⑥ Compensate: release hold — Inventory hold released before error returned to client — no orphaned locks.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant I as Inventory
    participant P as Payments
    participant S as Shipping
    C->>O: start checkout
    O->>I: reserve stock
    I-->>O: holdId
    O->>P: capture payment
    P-->>O: chargeId
    O->>S: create shipment
    S-->>O: trackingId
    O-->>C: order complete
body.caption: Forward saga steps from cart to shipment with durable orchestration.

body.source:
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant I as Inventory
    participant P as Payments
    O->>I: reserve stock
    I-->>O: holdId
    O->>P: capture payment
    P-->>O: FAILED
    O->>I: release hold (compensate)
    I-->>O: stock restored
body.caption: Payment failure triggers compensating release of inventory hold.

body.title: In practice
body.text: Temporal, AWS Step Functions, and Cadence power durable workflows at Uber, Netflix, and Stripe. Kafka-only choreography works until you need a human-readable workflow graph.

body.title: Why Temporal / Step Functions / Cadence?
body.text: Durable workflow state survives worker crashes. Timers (wait 7 days for KYC) without holding threads. Replay history for debugging failed checkouts.

body.title: Why Saga pattern (not 2PC)?
body.text: Payment, inventory, and shipping are separate services — XA transactions across Stripe and your DB don't exist. Compensating actions (release hold) are explicit.

body.title: Why Kafka (choreography option)?
body.text: Services react to events without a central orchestrator — looser coupling, harder to visualize. Many teams start with orchestration, add events for notifications.

body.title: Why Per-service PostgreSQL?
body.text: Each bounded context owns its data — inventory service DB, payment service ledger. Shared monolith DB creates deployment coupling.

body.title: Why Idempotent activity implementations?
body.text: Retries are guaranteed — charge API must accept same idempotency key, ship API must tolerate duplicate create with same correlation ID.

body.title: Why Dead-letter queue?
body.text: Poison messages (bad payload) isolate without blocking the whole saga — ops can inspect and replay.
